text: The surgeon performs a subcarinal lymph node dissection, carefully exposing the area while preserving the vagal branches. A dissection plane is created around the right main bronchus using a harmonic scalpel, which allows for clear separation of bronchial and nodal branches without the need for clips.

The final view after esophageal and lymph node dissection shows the surgical field from the neck to the diaphragm, indicating the removal of potential metastatic tissue. The surgeon requests gauze for the procedure.

The surgeon prepares to immobilize the esophagus for anastomosis, carefully avoiding damage to the nearby nerve. The nerve and esophagus are visually close, requiring precise manipulation to prevent nerve injury.

The final view shows the complete separation of the esophagus and removal of lymph nodes along the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves. The trachea and major airways remain undamaged, and the left hilum is fully explored. The left pleura is intact, and diaphragmatic lymph nodes have been completely removed. The next steps involve pulling up the stomach to create an anastomosis and opening the hiatus.
The surgeon shows the prepared stomach in the abdominal cavity, ready for the next step in the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon widens the hiatus by cutting a portion of the right crus, then pulls up the stomach graft. The suture site between the resected esophagus and the conduit is visible.
The surgeon assesses the resistance of the stomach graft visually, determining the limit of tension before stopping. The suture is then cut at the determined point.
The surgeon uses scissors to grasp and cut tissue around the tumor site, located approximately 27 centimeters from the inside, in preparation for anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the Levin tube, pulling it back to inspect the mucosa, which appears intact and free of any visible tumor.
